sentences.text: Yeah-- so, as I mentioned before, there were two organizations which supported the refugees, the Orthodox, and the Zionists.
The Orthodox were a small minority in Budapest.
In the countryside they were quite numerous, but less active politically.
The Zionists were a small minority among the non-orthodox.
The two organizations, the Orthodox [NON-ENGLISH], the Orthodox Committee for Refugees, and the Zionist Committee for Refugees, originally did not cooperate with each other.
And the reason for that was that Hungarian Orthodox leadership, which was extremely right-wing, did not approve of cooperating with non-Orthodox.
But as the news became worse and worse from the surrounding countries, there was a movement among the Orthodox, headed by my father, to cooperate with the Zionists.
And so he had a number of informal meetings with Dr.
Kasztner, who was the head of the Zionist Rescue Organization.

It went very slowly, because the Satmar rebbe, who was also among those who returned to Hungary from Romania with the Hitler decision of 1940, and who became the Rabbinic Supreme Authority in Hungarian orthodoxy, objected to any cooperation with the non-Orthodox.
And he's threatened my father with excommunication, with putting him into a herem, if he cooperated with the non-Orthodox.
So things are dragging on for a while.
Then interfered a friend of my father, the son of the chief rabbi of Budapest, chief Orthodox rabbi of Budapest-- [NON-ENGLISH].
He went to see the Satmar rebbe and explained to him that this was a question of life and death.
And that it was important to cooperate with the non-Orthodox.
And the Satmar rabbi relented.
And so from then on Dr.
Kasztner and my dad, and their respective committees, could work together.
And did you attend any of those meetings?
No, I was too young to attend the meetings.
I was a kid, I mean, this happened in 1942, and maybe late 1941, I'm not sure exactly.
Late 1941, early '42.
But what I did was, I attended the luncheons at-- [SIGHS] at our home.
And Dr.

Kasztner used to come for lunch fairly regularly on Sundays, when he would talk things over with my dad, and we all got acquainted with him, and where he kind of developed a liking for this young guy that I was.
And so I knew him quite well.
That's the story of my-- my knowing Dr.
Kasztner, well before there was a Kasztner transport.
Yes.
Maybe I didn't make it very clear before.
It was very difficult to get into the university, there were very few Jewish students.
A matter of fact, in 1943, when-- when I entered the university, there were a total of 48 Jewish students in all Hungarian universities, most of them at the Budapest University.
In order to get into the university one had to-- you have to have an excellent report card, that was a precondition.
But there were hundreds, and hundreds of people who had excellent report cards.
You had to have pull, political influence, which usually meant a member of the government, or a past member of the government, who would intervene, and would--
The gentleman I mentioned before.
Dr.
Bela Kenney, who's a former minister of the interior of Hungary, also a professor at the university-- Professor Kenney.
And he did whatever was required.
I'm not aware of the financial aspects.
I'm sure there were financial involvements.
A number of people had to be paid off, that was par for the course in Hungary.
But finally I wound up being accepted at the school of law, the law school-- law and political sciences.
It was interesting.
It-- it used to be-- there used to be Jew beating at Hungarian universities, ever since World War I.
But by the time I got to the university, the number of Jews at the university was so small that the majority dispensed with beating us.
And our seniors reported to us that in the '30s there used to be regular Jew beating, but there was none during the less than a year that I went to the university.
There was discrimination.
We had to sit in the last row, of the lectures that we attended.
That was not a requirement of the university-- the university was officially neutral.
And the university treated us like everybody else.
But there was the Hungarian Students' Association, which was extremely right-wing, and very anti-Semitic.
And they made it clear, on the first day that we attended the so-called reception ceremonies, that the Jews have to sit in the last row, unless they want to be beaten up.
And they said-- this was said publicly-- there was a big reception meeting, where the-- the rector of the school, [NON-ENGLISH], the president welcomed us, and a bunch of professors spoke.
Then they left and then came the representatives of the student association.
And their first statement was, are there any Jews here?
And there were three or four of us.
And they said, stand up, and we stood up.
They said, you always will sit in the last row, go up there to the last row.
And-- and then they started singing anti-Jewish songs, and that sort of thing.
But they didn't bother us, otherwise.
They-- there was no beating.
Anyway, that's the way I got to the university.
And I-- I attended the lectures and took my examinations.
The last examination I took in April, 1944, several weeks after the occupation, with the yellow star on my-- on my chest.

And-- because and the Germans came in, after German occupied Hungary on March 19, 1944, the school authorities announced that the schools would be closed starting in early April, as opposed to going through the semester until mid-June.
So they had examinations, and I went in and got myself examined.
And passed the examination.
That was the end of my-- my experience with university in Budapest.
The occupation occurred on March 19, 1944.
It was a Sunday.
It was a nice Sunday, actually.
I was studying Talmud with my teacher, who happened to be a Slovakian refugee.
I had a deal with my dad, which was that whereas he agrees that I should go to the university and there was no problem with that.
I agreed that I would continue to study Talmud, at least three times a week.
And the three times a week was on Shabbat, on Sunday, and some other day during the week.
So this was on Sunday morning and I was at my teacher's home.
And we were studying together when his wife came in and said, there is a rumor going around that the Germans are occupying Budapest.
I said, well, I-- I better get home and see what's going on.
We lived just one building off Parliament Square, which, Parliament Square is the largest square and most elaborate square in Budapest.
And Parliament Square is surrounded by-- by public buildings, one of them was the Ministry of Agriculture.
And our flat was in the house behind the Ministry of Agriculture.
In other words, we were separated from Parliament Square by the Ministry of Agriculture.
So I went home, which was a walk of about 15, 20 minutes-- I forgot whether I walked or whether I took to the streetcar-- and went out on Parliament Square.
And it was quite obvious that Budapest was occupied because [INAUDIBLE] there were German sentries in front of all the public buildings in Parliament Square.
So I went back to our house, the house where our flat was, we occupied the-- what you would call in this country, the third floor, we call it the second floor-- of the building.
That's where our flat was.
And found my mother and my brother, and want to get in touch with my father.
My father's headquarters was-- we were in Battery Street 4, his headquarters was in Battery Street 5.
That was the headquarters of his company.
But it was Sunday, and he wouldn't take the phone, and there wasn't anybody else in.
And so I couldn't get to him, so I ran over to 5, and rang the bell in the hope that he would answer.
But he didn't answer.
So I went back home, and we were all quite desperate.
We knew that something important was going on, and that Papa was locked in his office.
And we couldn't get to him.
I even tried to-- I-- I threw stones, little pebbles-- at his window-- the first floor.
But there was no reaction.
And so we were waiting, and-- and we were quite upset.
And then arrived the-- the Polish general-- forgot his name, I think it was [NON-ENGLISH], [?
Masovske, Mervske, something-- Masovske ?].
And he wanted to talk to my father, and my father wasn't there.
And-- so we were all waiting.
And-- then as he usually did, around 2:00 PM, he came home for a late lunch, and he didn't know anything.
And so we told him what happened.
And the general was very explicit, he said that, Mr.
Stern, tomorrow morning, the Germans will come at 5 o'clock in the morning to arrest you.
You should not be here by the time they come.
You got to go away somewhere.

They explained that that's not going to be too bad, because after a while the Germans are going to issue safety passes to anybody who was recommended by the-- by the Jewish Council, the so-called-- the Judenrat.
But that he had to disappear for a few days because, otherwise, he would be arrested.
So we packed up, and went to-- to-- called on the phone, and went to a brother of my father's.
Yeah, we all went-- left the place.
And the general was wrong, he didn't come on Monday morning, he came on Wednesday morning.
But we weren't home.
We stayed away for a week, and then after-- all kinds of things happened during that week, I just say it from the point of view of our family-- our little family.
Then [SIGHS] within eight or nine days, Dad got his protective pass, and then we could go back home.
But that was essentially our-- our reaction to the occupation.
Plus a number of other things like, the general told my dad, [INAUDIBLE] look, Mr.
Stern.
Arthur is 19 years old, he's an adult.
You've got to understand that he will have to stand on his own from now on.
You've got to give him a lot of money so he can buy himself out of any situation.
He should be able to buy himself out of a situation, which he says would usually arise as such, that he will be stopped by somebody in the street, either a German or a Hungarian.
They will ask him to go under the-- the entrance gate of a house, and they would ask him to take his pants off.
And once they-- in Hungary, there were only Jews who were circumcised.
At that point, he will have to have money.
And he will have to have money, not just to give, but also to promise.
So to tell the people that, here is money, and there will be more coming.
Anyway, my dad gave me five $1,000 bills at that moment, that afternoon.
I've never seen a $1,000 bill since that time.
Pardon?
No.
No, all kinds of things happened, but that one scene didn't that didn't take place.
But anyway, he gave me five $1,000 bills, things I have never seen before or after.
And he gave me 31,000 pengo bills.
Those were the Hungarian currency, which was roughly the same amount.
So I was running around with the equivalent of $10,000 in my pocket.
It became unsafe to be on the streets.
People were arrested [SIGHS] indiscriminately.
People disappeared indiscriminately.
The schools were closed down-- not just the university, all schools.
Jews are not permitted to go to the theater, to the movies, to public parks.
I think on April 5, we had to start wearing the Jewish stars-- the Jewish Star.
If you're caught-- if you were caught without wearing the Jewish Star, anything could happen depending on who caught.
You could be arrested, you could be shot, you know.
There was-- [SIGHS] there was random-- there were random incursions by-- by all kinds of bands, official, unofficial, German, Hungarian.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Just-- just people who decided that they wanted to benefit from the situation.
And--
I never got caught.
I-- Of course I was-- I was fortunate in the sense that I immediately had-- immediately, within a week or 10 days-- I had a protective pass that was from my father.
But then I went to work for the-- the Jewish Council, the Judenrat, and I had my own protective pass.
And--
Well, at the beginning there was confusion, nobody knew what to do.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it took-- it took weeks until it became clear what the Germans really expected from the Jewish Council.
Fundamentally, the Germans expected from the Jewish Council collaboration, and helping implementing whatever they wanted to do.
It was officially called the Jewish Council of Hungary.
But it really wasn't, because not even members of the Jewish Council could travel outside Budapest.
Jews who tried to travel outside Budapest were arrested, put into concentration camps, and deported.
We-- The council was really a Budapest Jewish Council.
It-- and in Budapest, it was expected to cooperate with the Germans, and to provide them with their needs.

And these needs ranged from, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 1,000 blankets within the next 24 hours for our soldiers to-- particularly apartments, lodging-- for soldiers, SS people, German civilians, all of whom streamed into Hungary and Budapest at that time.
I worked in the lodging department of the Jewish Council.
We were fairly highly prized people at the time-- the 48 full members of the Association of Hungarian Jewish University Students.

We were sort of considered, on the one hand, as being the cream-- I'm talking about, that's the way were considered by the Jewish community, the people who-- the young people who made it in these difficult circumstances.
We were all also the kids of rather influential and rich people.
So in the Jewish Council, we have-- we were given all kinds of key spots.
My initial assignment was to essentially help getting apartments for the Germans, apartments or houses, for the Germans, per their request.

The German request would get in around 6 o'clock in the morning to the Jewish Council, and they would say, we need 18 apartments of roughly two rooms, with one bathroom, and we need six flats, two bathrooms, and six bedrooms, or whatever, and we need two villas.
And then they would send us out, and-- that is the housing department, the lodging department of the council would send us out, always in groups of two.
And they would give us a bunch of addresses, because the council had the list of all Jewish homes in Budapest.
Or they thought they had the list of all Jewish homes.
These lists are never completely accurate.
And then we would go around and we would, by noon, have to come back and see-- we have notified the inhabitants of such and such apartment, or flat, or house, that they have to move out.
And this is what we have accomplished.
It was a fairly dirty job.
The Jewish Council had places for them to go, other Jewish homes, and apartments, and flats.
It was-- it was a very difficult job.
We were selected for it because we were considered being sufficiently tactful and well-educated to be able to do a-- a dirty job, fundamentally a very dirty job, in a-- and in a humane way.
And-- and by-- usually by 4 o'clock in the afternoon, the premises had to be vacated.
In other words, we would come in somewhere, typically between say, 7:30 and 11 o'clock.
Always two of us, sometimes three, never one.
We would notify the inhabitants that your apartment is being confiscated, and you have to move out by 4 o'clock this afternoon.
And this is the place where you go, and the people will be prepared to receive you because they have been notified.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That was-- that was the task at the beginning.
That is-- I would say that this started in probably early April and went through May.
Then in early June, or late May maybe, the situation changed.
At that point the ghettoization of Budapest was decided.
Now, the ghettoization of Budapest was very different from the ghettoization almost anywhere else.
I'm not aware of any other city where ghettoization was carried out like in Budapest.

Ghettos in Poland and Slovakia, and Romania, anywhere else, were closed areas of town, usually surrounded by a wall or by something else, and the Jews live-- and all the Jews live inside the ghetto, and the non-Jews live outside the ghetto.

In Budapest, largely because of fear of aerial attacks, the Hungarian authorities-- of course with the approval of the Germans, nothing was done by the Hungarians without the approval of the Germans-- decided to have not a single ghetto surrounded by a wall, but to have ghetto houses.
And a ghetto house would be a house in the middle of many other houses, which were not ghetto houses.
The ghetto house would house exclusively Jews, and all the Jews from the non-ghetto houses had to move out.
Now, what would be reasonably typical would be that a ghetto house, particularly in the non-Jewish areas of town, the mixed areas of town-- would be surrounded by five or six non-ghetto houses.
The ghetto houses, of course, represented a hardship for both parts, the Jewish and the non-Jewish part.

If a house was declared a ghetto house, then the non-Jews had to move out, and the-- I forgot what they were given, about a week-- and move elsewhere, usually in abandoned Jewish apartments elsewhere in town.
And most of them did well, they got a better apartment than they had before, a better flat than they had before.
And the Jews in turn had to move into the ghetto houses, and that was a high-density type situation.
Generally it was expected there would be three or four people per room in the-- in the ghetto houses.
No.
I was-- I was-- but I really sought an assignment to become the guy who would arrange things in our district.
So I-- I was assigned to be-- to head that district, and I designated the house in which we had our flat as a ghetto house, so that our non-Jewish neighbors had to move out.
And in turn, most members of our family, who-- who had to move out of what became non-ghetto houses, moved in with us.
And that took up-- that process took up probably the first three weeks of June.
And then very soon our transport, the Kasztner transport left on June 30.
The Kasztner transport is described more or less accurately in a number of pieces of literature.
But let me summarize it.
Basically Dr.
Kasztner established a relationship with the Germans very soon after March 19.
And the relationship was essentially based on Kasztner promising the Germans things that they wanted, and the Germans demanding things from Kasztner in exchange for Jewish lives.
The idea was, originally, that the Germans wanted 10,000 trucks.
And they said that for 10,000 trucks they would trade 1 million Jews.
That was the statement made by Eichmann, who was the chief executioner of the Germans, and the head of the so-called Juden Kommando-- the Jewish Command of the Germans in Budapest.
And who, incidentally, had been in charge of evacuating and killing Jews for many years.
And you know about the Eichmann trial that took place in-- in Israel.
Eichmann essentially-- partly directly, partly indirectly-- Eichmann personally resented dealing with Jews.
So he usually did it through his associates, Becher or Krumey.
And Becher or Krumey, sometimes in the presence of Eichmann, proposed a deal of 10,000 trucks for 1 million Jews.
In the meantime they deported and killed Jews, there were no million Jews available to-- and there's the long story that I don't want to go into because I don't really know the details of it.

But finally, what it amounted to was that-- that, as a gesture of goodwill, as a gesture of goodwill, Eichmann, with the full knowledge of Himmler-- Himmler was, whoever he was, you know-- offered to take in a transport of 600 Hungarian Jews, to take them into a protective camp.
And from there, via Spain, to deliver them to Palestine.
